title: An IQP Born Machine for Calorimeter Image Generation at 64 Qubits with Compiled-IQP Deployment
authors: Jamal Slim, Saverio Monaco, Florian Rehm, Dirk Krücker, Kerstin Borras
abstract: We train an instantaneous quantum polynomial-time (IQP) Born machine on real high-energy-physics calorimeter shower images at $64$ qubits and compile the trained model into a single sampling-hard IQP circuit for quantum deployment. The pipeline has three components: a Mixture-of-IQP (\moiqp{}) architecture, whose Walsh-diagonal MMD$^{2}$ loss is classically trainable by Van den Nest Fourier Monte Carlo; the Pearson-Stabilized Correlation Kernel (\psck{}), a positive-definite MMD kernel that biases descent toward correlation-sensitive directions through a data-evaluated Jacobian of the empirical Pearson matrix; and an exact deferred-measurement compilation of \moiqp{} into a single IQP circuit on $\nfeat + \lceil \log_2 \Lcomp \rceil$ qubits (\ciqp{}). Across five seeds at $\Lcomp = 8$, $1500$ epochs, the model reaches $\maerho = 0.069 \pm 0.008$ against a $0.052$ encoding-fidelity floor on the training split and $0.071 \pm 0.008$ on a held-out test split, versus a Liu--Wang baseline at $\maerho = 0.100$. The compiled \ciqp{} reproduces the \moiqp{} marginal to $0.591 \pm 0.012$ times the Monte Carlo noise floor.
summary: This paper presents a new quantum generative model designed to produce high-resolution calorimeter images for high-energy physics. By using a specialized correlation-sensitive loss function and a compilation technique, the authors achieve high-fidelity generation at 64 qubits without encountering barren plateaus.
main_problem: Developing a scalable quantum generative model for high-resolution (64-qubit) calorimeter image generation while overcoming the limitations of existing IQP models in capturing spatial correlations and avoiding barren plateaus.
main_result: The PSCK-MoIQP model significantly outperforms the Liu-Wang baseline in correlation reconstruction and demonstrates that the compiled cIQP circuit can be deployed on quantum hardware with sub-Monte-Carlo noise precision.
method: A 'train-on-classical, deploy-on-quantum' workflow using a Pearson-Stabilized Correlation Kernel (PSCK) for training and a Walsh-Hadamard-based deferred-measurement compilation (cIQP) for deployment.
model_or_system: A Mixture-of-IQP (MoIQP) architecture consisting of L independent IQP circuits sharing an Erdős-Rényi gate graph, which is compiled into a single larger IQP circuit (cIQP) using ancilla qubits.
key_observables: Pauli-Z words/correlators, Pearson correlation matrices, Mean Absolute Error of correlations (MAE_rho), and Z-marginals.
important_parameters: 64 qubits (n=64), 8 mixture components (L=8), 8 bits per feature (B=8), and an Erdős-Rényi gate graph with average degree 6.
main_assumptions: The PSCK loss uses a Gauss-Newton linearization which is exact at the optimum; the compilation assumes the ability to implement deferred measurement via ancilla qubits.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the MoIQP and cIQP architectures, compare Pearson correlation residuals between models, show per-feature distributional agreement, and plot training trajectories and gradient variance scaling.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scientific challenge of HEP image simulation, describes the MoIQP and cIQP architectures, details the PSCK loss function and training algorithm, presents numerical results on 64-qubit scaling and correlation accuracy, and concludes with a verification of the compilation fidelity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Overall pipeline. Raw calorimeter shower data r ∈RD is quantile-binned and binary-encoded to bitstrings x ∈{0, 1}n with n = D · B. The MoIQP model is trained on classical hardware through Van den Nest Fourier Monte Carlo [20] evaluation of its low-order Pauli-Z correlators with the PSCK MMD objective. After training, the L components are compiled into a single IQP circuit on n + a qubits with a = ⌈log2 L⌉Walsh–Hadamard ancillas (cIQP); this compiled circuit is the object deployed on quantum hardware to generate calorimeter samples. The classical–quantum boundary separates training (Van den Nest MC) from deployment (Bremner–Jozsa–Shepherd-hard sampling).

FIG. 2: Single IQP Born-machine circuit. The Hadamard–diagonal–Hadamard sandwich is the defining structure of IQP circuits. The diagonal block D(θ) = Q G∈G exp(i θG Q i∈G Zi) gives one Z-phase rotation per gate G of the graph: a single-qubit RZ for weight-1 gates, a ZZ-phase rotation (CNOT–RZ–CNOT, or a native cross-resonance ZX) for weight-2 gates. Measurement in the computational basis yields samples from pθ(x) = |⟨x|U(θ)|0⟩|2.

FIG. 3: MoIQP as a uniform mixture of IQP Born distributions. A single IQP circuit U(θ) = H⊗nD(θ)H⊗n on a shared gate graph G is instantiated with L independent angle vectors θ(ℓ). Each instance has Born distribution pℓ(x) = |⟨x|U(θ(ℓ))|0⟩|2, and the MoIQP model distribution is their uniform average pMoIQP(x) = 1

FIG. 4: cIQP deployment. The L-component MoIQP is compiled into a single IQP-form circuit on n + a qubits with a = ⌈log2 L⌉ancillas. The ancillas are prepared in the uniform Walsh–Hadamard superposition |+⟩⊗a = (1/ √

FIG. 5: Training-split MAEρ as a function of epoch at n = 64. Solid red: PSCK seed mean across the five training seeds. Shaded band: seed-wise min–max envelope at each epoch. Orange: single-seed Liu–Wang baseline at the same L = 8 and epoch budget. Dashed line: encoding-fidelity floor on the training split (0.052). The step features every 100 epochs are cosine restarts of Adam.
